10/10

The only thing I dislike about this game is that there isn't an infinite amount of it for me to enjoy.  I return to this game every few months, lose myself in it for a week, gush about how great it is to my friends, and then go back to the coldbox of other games like Bartmoss for another few months.

My only real gripes are that I wish there were more voice options, and I'd like a native option to narrow the field of random stats that populate when upgrading cyberware to stats that are relevant to your build.  Mods solve this problem handily, but it's something I hope they CDPR thinks about doing if this game gets a sequel.  Also, I personally am not a fan of any of the romance targets in the game; they all have some pretty crippling character flaws that would make me shy away from a serious relationship with them, so most of the time I just skip romance entirely.

9.5/10, sell me another one please -- preferably with fewer on-launch issues than this one.

A epic open world first person game with many different factions and political elites. A great story that'll have you either laughing or emotional at times. There's a skill tree mechanic in which you can unlock new abilities that'll either help you in combat, survive or learn skills to help you progress by means of learning how to hack systems, open doors and ventilation shafts.

The character animations are nicely done and life-like. The quests or missions are super long compared to other games. You literally feel like you're hanging out with someone for 2-3 hours. With lengthy dialogues and animations. Also, whether you are picking someone up, driving to a location to gather supplies, checking out or gathering intel on an enemy base before entering, driving to a location to pick up your reward. And even after everything, sometimes dropping that person back off.

To make the missions faster and easier to navigate, you can either fast travel to your objectives or allow AI to drive you to your objective markers with auto-drive. You can also skip forward in time, which skips dialogue and animations for those who have to reload a save and don't want to go through it all over again.

The Phantom Liberty DLC is well worth the price as I thought it would never end. But in a good way. With much more content, such as additional missions, skill tree, tech, cyberware, vehicles and weapons. A new cast of characters and actors. Not only does the base game alone have multiple different endings based on the decisions you make in the game, but the Phantom Liberty DLC also has many different endings.

The only downside to the game for me is there's no vehicle garage or aircraft hangars where you can customize your vehicles. You can at least purchase many different types of vehicles and give them a custom paint job.

I recommend making a manual save for each mission you play as there are some missable achievements and you also may have not liked the choices you have made that will change the overall experience and ending.

[b]Don't Forget To Grab The Free DLC Bonus Content :[/b]
- Original score
- Art booklet featuring a selection of art from the game
- Digital Comic Cyberpunk 2077: Your Voice
- Cyberpunk 2020 sourcebook
- Wallpapers for desktop and mobile

[b]I Recommend This Guide :[/b]
https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=2314731948
[b]Features :[/b]
- Photo Mode : When enabled in game, allows you to freely roam with a camera. It has many different features from changing camera settings, filters, adding / removing NPCs, adding backgrounds, overlays, stickers and much more. You can also make the camera live and not just a freeze frame still picture by changing the game speed.

- Apartments : You can purchase different apartments located throughout Night City where you can store items, check your computer, watch TV, shower, sleep and more.

- Relationships : You can choose from various main story characters in which you can have a relationship. There are different interactions based on the chosen apartment.

[b]Save Folder Location :[/b]
The saves can be found in the "CD Projekt Red" folder, which is located in :

C:\Users\USERNAME\Saved Games\CD Projekt Red

Awesome game, a bit misunderstood and overhated at launch.

Graphics are incredible overall, especially after all the updates. Night City is one of the most detailed and immersive open worlds ever made, full of life, neon lights, crowded streets and beautiful environments. Character models and facial animations are excellent, and the combat animations feel fluid and satisfying. Some NPC behaviors can still feel a bit awkward at times, but the overall presentation is top tier.

Sound design is amazing. Weapons sound powerful, cars feel aggressive, the city ambience is immersive, and the voice acting is top tier across the board. The music is absolutely phenomenal, perfectly matching every moment, whether you're cruising through the city at night, fighting through chaos, or experiencing emotional story moments.

Gameplay is great and highly engaging. Let's start with the combat, because many people still think Cyberpunk is just "GTA with RPG elements", but it offers so much more than that. There are many different playstyles to choose from: stealth hacking, aggressive gunplay, melee combat, katanas, throwing knives, cyberware-focused builds, snipers, shotguns, netrunning, and more. Every build feels different and has strengths and weaknesses depending on your approach and preparation. Combat is fast, fluid and rewarding, especially once your build starts coming together. The movement also feels great, especially with double jumps, dashes and cyberware upgrades that make you feel increasingly powerful.
The progression system is great, allowing you to specialize heavily into your preferred playstyle. Skill trees offer meaningful upgrades that genuinely change how you approach combat and exploration. Cyberware customization is one of the best parts of the game, letting you turn your character into an unstoppable machine, stealth hacker, samurai or whatever style you prefer.
Looting and equipment are also very satisfying. Finding iconic weapons, upgrading cyberware and experimenting with builds keeps the gameplay fresh for dozens of hours.
Exploration is another huge strength. Night City feels alive and dense, with hidden events, side gigs, random encounters and environmental storytelling everywhere. Even just driving around the city at night can be immersive on its own.
The side content is surprisingly strong too. Many side quests are written with the same quality as the main story, often presenting difficult choices, morally gray situations and memorable characters. Some side quests end up being more emotionally impactful than entire main campaigns in other games.
Another great mechanic is how your choices and relationships affect quest outcomes, dialogue and endings. Some opportunities, conversations and endings are only available depending on how you played and who you connected with throughout the game.
These are only some of the great mechanics the game offers, the rest are for you to discover.

The enemies are varied and fun to fight. Different gangs use different weapons, tactics and cyberware, forcing you to adapt your approach. Boss fights and cyberpsychos can also be very memorable, especially when your build is tested properly.

The story is excellent, emotional and extremely well written. The characters are memorable, believable and full of personality. V and Johnny Silverhand have fantastic chemistry throughout the game, and many side characters leave a strong impression too. The role playing aspects are very well done, with meaningful dialogue choices, different character backgrounds and multiple endings depending on your decisions. The game constantly presents morally complex situations where there is rarely a completely right or wrong answer. Also some of the endings are brutal, so be prepared to a rollercoaster of emotions.

In short, awesome game, a bit misunderstood because of its disastrous launch. After years of updates and improvements, the game became what people originally hoped it would be. Many people still judge it based on old impressions without giving it another chance. If you enjoy immersive RPGs, great storytelling, strong world building and flexible character progression, this game is absolutely worth experiencing.

In the last few days before Cyberpunk's day one release, I told a buddy of mine how excited I was and that I was going to buy it day one. He warned me up and down and I listened to him. And good thing I did.

5 years later, when I upgraded my rig, Cyberpunk was the game I used to test it. And it was everything I had hoped it would be on day one.

Night City really is the Rapture of the 2020s. That experience alone is worth every penny.

Well, after 86 hours I finally 100%’d the game  - all side quests, fixer gigs, DLC, main story, everything you can possibly do in one playthrough without save scumming.

So, is Cyberpunk 2077 worth the money right now? Yeah, it is. What it was at launch and what it is today are basically two completely different games. It’s awesome that they didn’t just drop it like so many devs do nowadays.

The only thing I’ll say is I would never call this a real RPG. It’s got about as much RPG as The Witcher 3. While you’re on the story or doing quests, the world feels alive. But the second you step away from the main path, everything goes dead. The world is also pretty empty content-wise, even though it looks ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ gorgeous, perfect for screenshots.

I'm more of a medieval fantasy fan over sci-fi and I love the Witcher universe, but Cyberpunk did a great job of getting me invested in this world. Great gameplay, story and my favourite open world city, the dlc is just as good if not better. 

The one thing I found it lacked was long term consequence. There's some, but depending on which ending you choose those options may not matter anyway. It's the one thing Witcher 3 did far better and I hope Cyberpunk 2 improves on it. 

Still an easy must play.

Cyberpunk 2077 has its problems, but despite that, the game remains one of the best story-driven experiences I've ever had. The atmosphere itself - when you're just wandering around Night City - is incredibly immersive. This city feels real, contrasting, and alive, it completely absorbs you. It's a rare case where you want to keep coming back here again and again, just for that sense of presence.

When this game first came out I refused to buy it it because of the state it was in. 6 years later I was able to get it on sale for like 15 dollars and thought why not. Overall, was playable, though still buggy. I like the story and the world, and the gameplay. But mostly the gameplay of sidequests and main stories. The boss fights are really not that great as is the melee combat. And especially bosses that primarily do melee or have knockdown attacks suck. But that's not really what the game was trying to go for anyway. 

Keanu Reeves carries hard. The game is definitely worth playing, and while flawed, is very fun, and has great worldbuilding behind it too.

/SPOILER WARNING IN PARAGRAPH 2/

I honestly am torn between recommending and not recommending this game. The gameplay itself is fantastic. It makes a lot of fun, and that is only after one playthrough of a more stealth/Quickhack based Playstyle. The looting, scenery and also just the overall atmosphere is great and brings everything I want from a Sci-fi Looter Shooter. 

My Problem is with the Endings. I feel they are all badly written and just make no sense at all. I chose the Arasaka Ending, or rather came out there where I did the first one at the rooftop without the Pills, restarted and used the other longer one where I go to the Arasaka Tower and play through the Hanako Story. 

The Ending I got there is just terrible. My Romance just ups and leaves after I called her from the Orbital station and I die 6 months after. Or I would have chosen the other route of just surrendering myself to Mikoshi, completely ignoring the entire Alt cunningham arc where I thought something happens since she told me to get into Mikoshi and she helps me after. (Did not play that ending, but from  all I read it does not end well either and you just die?)

The Endings are understandably not all roses and sunshine as is NightCity standard, but they feel forcibly bad and not coherent with the characters and the previous storytelling. 

Takes away from a great game and also makes it infuriatingly bad for a decision making game to feel like no decisions make a difference or get you to your outcome.

Overall I recommend the game for the Play aspect and surely the replayability with difference Endings, Approaches or Romance and Storylines.
Just be prepared that the Ending might leave you disappointed or feel taken out of the immersion.

It's absolutely one of the best games i've ever played, the mechanics are so fun, the graphics are even so good on the lowest settings
Sadly i had to refund it because i had lag spikes every 16 seconds (especially in night city) but when i upgrade my pc or buy a new one i am DEFINITYLY getting this masterpiece again
peak story, peak gameplay, peak characters, peak atmosphere. absolute gem. everyone should play this game once in their lifetime. ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ love it

An entertaining and depressing glimpse into the future with a cast of very compelling characters and an immersive world that reflects a lot of the darkest aspects of our society taken to their logical extremes. The combat is satisfying, though can get pretty easy at times. This emotional rollercoaster of a game feels extremely prophetic and will remain relevant for decades to come

First bought the game when it was released. Then still full of bugs, but later very much made better by patches and especially Dogtown in Phantom Liberty. I don't play much shooter games, but this game if defenitly part of my top 5 favorite games all time due to the many different ways you can play it. The storyline is very deep and very detailed, you can unlock many different things each playthrough and you can play it how you want to play it with a fitting end to your style of game. 

When I started my first playthrough, currently just 120 hours ago, I played the base game first which I completely loved. Later, when I "finished" the basegame, I bought Phantom LIberty and played that through. 

TL;DR:
Fantastic game, 10/10 would play again the rest of my life.

Seriously. If you can run this game on Max setting with 100 fps+, this game is real ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ amazing.

First session after installation I got 5 hours straight, Graphic is great, like my PC turned to heat oven fan burst great.
great gunplay and support for variety of action playstyles. Tons of great additions through free updates as well

I spent half the game arguing with Johnny. I spent the whole game missing Jackie.
Best gameplay I've seen in a long time. The story is hit or miss as it's really only the Prologue and the Ending missions that I felt any sort of narrative being expressed.

Not askin' you to never give up. Sometimes you gotta let go...

Even when it was released and it was called a buggy mess, i never had issues and loved the games. Ever since then the game has only gotten better. Playthrough after playthrough i keep coming back to one of the best RPGs of the modern age. This is one of the few games I'm happy to have 100%'ed and one of the few games i regret not buying the collector's edition of.
liked it enough to 100% it. the story and worldbuilding are great even on my sixth playthrough. the gameplay/combat is also awesome ever since 2.0.

There are few bugs, and none have tainted the game for me. This is a great game and I wish it was released like this because it would have been a legendary success. Great story. Great feel. I just want more and more detail. Can't wait for the next one, but I only buy games on sale now long after they release. It's the only way you know what you are getting into anymore.

There are so many things I like about this game, but the biggest is that it makes me feel better about myself as a software developer. Stacked objects will randomly fling themselves around, especially tires. Traffic randomly stops or collide, and vehicles tear off for no reason. Bodies get stretched or thrown off into the far distance. It's fun to jump into a beautiful cityscape and wreak havoc, constantly being reminded that it's just a game.
For the actual game, I'd give it a B+ these five years after its launch. It's far, far too cutscene driven. The main storyline is just small side quests between long cutscenes. I constantly press 'skip forward' to advance dialog. I've completed all side jobs and quests, gotten all of the rewards from the fixers, and completed 3 of 4 game endings, but what I really remember are the early story arcs and the feeling that I didn't really know which subplot to follow (Johnny? Takemura? Panam? Evelyn? Judy? Rogue? River?).
In that way it was like Elden Ring to me. Confusing, fun, bewildering, and eventually something I'm glad I mastered. Night City is a fun place to visit, but I wouldn't want to live there.
This game is just a preem experience. great story, open world, side quests the list could go on. it will change your life, and I feel sad now that I have to delta out of this world.
Great storyline, great gameplay, small bugs but does not ruin the experience

Right, 400+ hours in Night City and I’m still clocking in like it’s a part-time job I never applied for. That alone should tell you everything.

Cyberpunk 2077 had one of the messiest launches I’ve ever seen in gaming. Proper chaos. Bugs everywhere, NPCs glitching like they were having a breakdown, cars spawning inside buildings, whole internet clowning on it for months. It was rough. Not even gonna sugarcoat it.

But here’s the mad part — underneath all that mess, there was actually a brilliant game trying to get out. And after all the updates, fixes, overhauls, DLC polish… it finally did.

Now? It’s a completely different beast.

⸻

You play V, just trying to make a name in Night City. Simple enough on paper. Get rich, survive, maybe get out. Standard dream. Except Night City doesn’t do “simple”. It chews you up and laughs while doing it.

Everything kicks off with a job that’s meant to be clean, quick money. Obviously it goes sideways instantly — because of course it does. And that’s where Johnny Silverhand shows up in your head like the world’s most aggressive unwanted roommate.

And from that point on, the whole story shifts.

It stops being just “V’s story” and becomes this constant tug-of-war between two minds in one body. You want survival. Johnny wants chaos, revenge, and to burn everything down just for the vibe of it. You’re trying to hold yourself together while this rockstar terrorist ghost is constantly in your ear judging your life choices like he’s got any moral high ground.

And the best part? It doesn’t stay one-note. Their relationship actually develops. At first it’s pure hate and sarcasm. Then it becomes tolerance. Then this weird, messy understanding. And eventually… something in-between that’s honestly hard to label.

It’s not just “buddy cop in your brain”. It’s more like slowly losing parts of yourself while arguing with someone who refuses to shut up. Proper mental.

⸻

Side characters? Genuinely some of the best written in modern gaming. Every major story arc feels like it actually matters. No filler nonsense just to stretch runtime. People in Night City are all trying to survive in their own way, and most of them are just as cooked as you are — they just hide it differently.

And the game is constantly reminding you: there are no clean endings here. Just different shades of bad and worse.

⸻

Gameplay-wise, after 400 hours I’ve basically turned V into every possible flavour of unhinged.

Sneaky netrunner ghost build? Done it.
Katana cyber-psycho sprinting through enemies? Done it.
Walking tank with more chrome than humanity? Also done it.

Night City itself is still ridiculous. Neon everywhere, rain reflecting off concrete, ads screaming at you 24/7. It’s beautiful but in a slightly depressing, overstimulating way. Like a city that never lets you breathe properly.

AI can still act a bit cooked sometimes, not gonna lie. You’ll see some NPCs doing the most questionable behaviour known to man. But honestly at this point it just feels like part of the city’s personality.

⸻

Here’s the weird bit — after that long, you stop playing it like a normal game. You just… exist in Night City. You know the streets. You know the shortcuts. You know exactly which gigs are gonna end in chaos before they even start. And yet you still do them.

Because it’s not really about progression anymore. It’s about the vibe. The atmosphere. The feeling of being permanently one bad decision away from disaster.

And Johnny in your head never shuts up. Ever. Commenting on everything like an uninvited backseat driver with anger issues.

⸻

Cyberpunk 2077 isn’t perfect. Still got its rough edges, still janky in places, still occasionally does something that makes you pause and go “what on earth just happened”.

But when it hits, it really hits.

It’s a story about identity, control, mortality, and slowly losing yourself while pretending you’re still in charge.

And after 400 hours?

Yeah… I’m still coming back.

Because I swear down at this point I’m just simping for Johnny Silverhand and I don’t even have the energy to deny it anymore.

Before i get into im not gonna harp on anything the game does bad in this cuz i feel that the good outweighs it so much more.

Let me just say the launch drama tricked me like fr I can’t believe I slept on this for so long I literally avoided this game for years because of the terrible launch memes. I finally bought it on Steam a while back but never played it until it took the last 3-4 days of my life the past couple days because i was grinding it and I’m actually blown away. I feel so stupid for waiting this long to play it. If you’re still hesitating because of stuff from 2020, just buy it. On PC, it runs super smooth (besides when i share my screen on dc), looks insane, and is easily one of the most immersive games out. Also if you have watched the anime boy are you gonna love this game 
8/10 overall (Story is a solid 9/10 though)

Night City is a dystopian masterpiece
The city isn't just a map it is a living character that suffocates you with its vibe.
The scale is overwhelming you look up and see massive megabuildings casting shadows over neon drenched alleys packed with vendors, cyberpunks, and garbage.
Kabuki and Japantown feel claustrophobic and alive with bright neon signs and dense crowds.
Pacifica feels like a warzone completely isolated from corporate control. 
The Badlands offer a massive, dust choked contrast where you can just throttle willy nilly on a bike. The environmental storytelling is everywhere with much more to find  then you think if you look around for stuff. 
these are just a few of many places i listed.

as for the gameplay The gameplay hits a super satisfying rhythm because it stops playing like a standard shooter and lets you become an absolute monster. The gunplay is heavy and punchy, especially when using tech weapons that punch through solid concrete walls or smart guns that track targets around corners.
The build variety is where the game actually excels

The Sandevistan build lets you activate a neural implant to slow down time, letting you run circles around enemies and slice them to pieces with a katana before they can even draw their weapons.
Mantis Blades and Gorilla Arms turn your own body into a lethal weapon, making close-quarters combat incredibly brutal.
Quickhack builds turn you into a ghost, allowing you to wipe out an entire building of guards without firing a shot by forcing their cyberware to self detonate or burn them alive from the inside out. or even kill themself 
theres many more builds out there these are just a few

The side quests aren't just filler either major storylines involving characters like Judy, Panam, or River feel just as fleshed out and emotional as the main plot. Vehicle handling takes some getting used to, and some of the minor fixer gigs can feel a bit repetitive, which is why the gameplay is an 8/10 for me. But when you are fully kitted out with cyberware and sliding through a firefight, the power trip is unmatched.

One thing you notice pretty quickly is that the clothing options are totally unbalanced. If you play as a guy, the male fashion choices feel lowkey mid and kinda trash. Meanwhile, the female clothing options get all the drip everything looks amazing, stylish, and actually fits the futuristic cyberpunk aesthetic perfectly. It wasn't an issue for me at all because I always prefer playing as a girl in most games anyway, but if you’re a dude who wants to play as a male V and look cool, the lack of good male drip is definitely going to annoy you.


The absolute best part of the game imo is Johnny Silverhand.
At first, I lowkey thought he was just a loud annoying terrorist.
I hated having this toxic digital ghost constantly trash talk me and all my choices i made (even if you drive to fast) most interactions force you to choose to follow with or against his radical philosophy that could cost you your life.
He felt like a nuisance stuck in V's head. He pops up as a hologram out of nowhere to talk ♥♥♥♥ on all the people you talk to. (they cant see or hear him)
But the writing is and gets so deep that your relationship actually evolves.
The more I played, the more I actually started to understand him.
His tragic backstory and cynical worldview completely won me over. not to mention his amazingly curated personality 
Hes an ego driven rockstar who masks his massive regrets with arrogance and dark humor and he holds up a mirror to the darkest parts of Night City, making you question who the real villains are.
but the friction slowly turns into understanding. You watch this deeply flawed, egoist slowly lower his guard and show real humanity. The transition happens so naturally that you don't even realize how close you've become until it all hits you. He goes from a parasite wanting you dead to someone who genuinely puts his own existence on the line just to save your life. By the end, they share an unbreakable bond out of shared trauma and mutual respect. Their banter, their quiet moments of vulnerability, and that shift from bitter enemies to true brothers-in-arms (i played as a girl) is easily one of my favorite story arcs in video game history. Keanu Reeves and the writing team absolutely cooked. 
Johnny Silverhand is now my favorite side character of all time and Honestly, calling him a "side character" feels like an insult. He is literally the co-protagonist. His dynamic with V is the beating heart of the whole story. Keanu Reeves ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ cooked with this performance.

Pretty solid AAA game, i wish they provided more context for every major decision you gotta make, the game kind of expects you to have played the whole thing already and gives you no info to make the choice you actually want. Stealth is top tier, regular combat is a bit awkward, melee is pretty janky and netrunning is ridiculously OP lol, id honestly avoid it next time bc it makes the game too easy.

My main complaint would be the endings, there are some VERY specific decisions you gotta make to enable all the endings, good thing is you can always choose which one to play and you can play them all, as long as you fulfilled the somewhat secret prerequisites. That said tho, the game literally tells you that *someone* cant be saved and doesnt even provide an (optional) side objective so i though it was true but the game purposely lied to me so now my 100hs+ save is going to be permanently lacking a character i actually liked and favored the entire playthrough. Im not gonna go through 70hs of gameplay AGAIN just bc of this, luckly i found an autosave there and will get the achievement but still, the game is pretty lacking in warning the player ''hey this stuff is important, heres some info for you to make an informed decision'', instead its like ''LMAO just start the whole game again and learn this next time, maybe''.

It really sucks bc i had to play the entire thing with the wiki opened on the second monitor, frecuently got spoiled and its FANDOM. Yes they didnt move to wiki.gg, my ram cries everytime.

Performance wise its pretty well optimised for today standards, its mostly CPU bound tho so if you have a decent GPU you'll be able to have most settings maxed with raytracing disabled, my frames drop to 75 worst case bc cpu, if not im usually over 100 sometimes even capped at 144 bc gsync.

I've never cried so much to every ending in a game before, never felt so happy to be immersed inside of a games world, never adored the writing and characters + relationships they all have with each other, never had so much fun in 3d combat, never got so much enjoyment and meaning and escape from a game before than Cyberpunk 2077. Truly this game went from being barely playable on all platforms outside of mediocre on PC in December 2020 to being one of the greatest video games of all time. But even switching from a 3060 to a 5070 to a 7900XTX to a 7800XT to now a 4070ti Super my experience never felt terrible, granted its always felt better since after my 3060. I'll probably never find a game that connects to my struggles in life again so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ much and that honestly makes me sad, outside of the fact Ill probably play this game forever. Thank you CD Projekt RED, please don't ruin the next game, I'll never be able to forget the work and dedication your team put into adapting the world into this game, beautifully executing it, messing up, and then not only fixing everything in stride but releasing one of the best DLCs for a game ive ever played. Seeya in the major leagues 😞.

I believe i originally preordered this game way back in 2018, before it was delayed multiple times. By the time it came out, i wasn't even too broken up over the rough state the game was in. After years of waiting, the money i had spent on it hardly felt like it had ever existed. I played the game for 10 hours and then dropped it.

Fast Forward to 2026 and i get the urge to come give it another shot. I remembered enjoying the bit of the story I did get to experience, and i knew that the gameplay and bugginess of the game had been improved to various extents.

Having now binged nearly all of the content in the base game over the course of two weeks, i can confidently say that i am satisfied with my purchase of this game eight years ago. 
While occasionally rough around the edges, i did thoroughly enjoy the combat and character progression of the game. Running mostly a blade build let me enjoy an up close, frenetic combat style that i imagine to be wholly different but equally as enjoyable as to what might be experienced from focusing more on quickhacks or on smart weapons.
I found myself doing nearly all of the side jobs the game had to offer (save for the gigs, but i occasionally still did those if i stumbled into them), and was well satisfied with more of them than i would have expected. This is really all thanks to the character writing in this game. 

By far, the standout feature of Cyberpunk is its writing. Dialogue between characters feels natural and animations in important moments are incredibly well done. I found myself really invested in the relationships between many of the main cast, and especially the relationships that V has with Johnny, Panam and Judy. 
I won't speak on the endings too much, but i think they are all very well written. Something tells me that people were not happy with some of them when the game came out, but maybe i'm just being harsh on the average gamer in 2020.

I will say, the performance in the open world can sometimes be lacking, although i will admit that i run the game on ultra settings with a 2k monitor and average above 60 fps. I will especially mention that I never had any performance hiccups during important story scenes, I never once found myself taken out of a moment because of lag. The game also has occasional small bugs, like a cop walking around inside the sidewalk or a civilian loudly and repeatedly running their car into a wall 20 feet away from a conversation you're having. Nothing serious.

Honestly, my biggest complaint with the game is the content surrounding the romantic relationships in the game, or the lack of it. [spoiler] I can only speak on the relationship with Judy, but i imagine the others are quite the same. There's a whole big mission (and honestly a decent amount of setup in the background throughout the game) of this potential relationship blossoming, and once it finally does, the only thing that comes of it is occasional text conversations that eventually dry up, and a repeatable date mission in which you have access to a few small events and a set of dialogue that never changes and is largely the same as what you would get when you called Judy before you were even in a relationship. [/spoiler]
I think it speaks greatly to this game's strengths that this is my biggest issue with it.

I know this game has largely positive reviews today, but i imagine there are still a lot of people who view the game in the light it was (often justly) received in at launch (although there were certainly unrealistic expectations surrounding the game at the time as well). I hope any of those people who may have dropped the game like i did come give it another shot some time, it's really worth it.

I think I'd give this game a 9/10. I'm very happy I came back to it. Really looking forward to the DLC next time it goes on sale :)

This game has come so far from its release debacle. With the bugs fixed and game optimization you can focus on the story, gameplay, upgrades, etc, if you love roleplaying story driven games this is the holy grail.

Few games in history have undergone a redemption arc quite like Cyberpunk 2077. While its initial launch was famously rocky, CD Projekt RED’s unwavering commitment to their vision—culminating in the massive 2.0 update and the brilliant Phantom Liberty expansion—has officially transformed this game into a certified masterpiece.

Night City is the undisputed star of the show. It is a towering, neon-soaked vertical labyrinth that feels genuinely alive, dangerous, and intoxicating. Walking through the crowded streets of Watson or driving through the desolate Badlands offers an unparalleled level of environmental immersion. The world-building is incredibly dense, capturing the corporate-dominated, gritty essence of classic cyberpunk lore.

It is impossible to talk about the renaissance of this game without mentioning Cyberpunk: Edgerunners, the phenomenal Studio Trigger anime series. Edgerunners didn't just capture the hearts of fans; it perfectly mirrored the game's high-stakes, tragic, and adrenaline-fueled atmosphere.

The anime injected a massive wave of fresh energy into the game, making the exploration of Night City feel even more personal. Visiting locations featured in the show, listening to "I Really Want to Stay at Your House" on the in-game radio, or equipping David Martinez’s iconic jacket adds a profound layer of emotional depth for anyone who loved the series.

With the overhauled cyberware systems, revamped skill trees, and chaotic vehicle combat introduced in recent updates, the gameplay finally matches the narrative's ambition. Combat is fast, brutal, and highly customizable. Whether you want to be a stealthy Netrunner hacking enemies from afar or a sandevistan-powered solo slicing through corporate guards with a katana, the playstyles are incredibly rewarding.

At its core, the story of V and Johnny Silverhand is a deeply moving tale about legacy, identity, and survival against all odds.

Cyberpunk 2077 is no longer just a game about promises; it is a triumphant delivery of an unforgettable sci-fi RPG. If you were waiting for the right time to lose yourself in Night City—especially if you are a fan of Cyberpunk: Edgerunners—there has never been a better time to chrome up and dive in.

It had a shaky start, but after a few updates and an incredible DLC, this has become one of the best rpgs ever made.  The characters, writing, and worldbuilding are excellent, and there's enough content to keep you engaged for dozens if not hundreds of hours.  And if you ever get bored, there's an incredible amount of content available through mods that can transform this into an entirely different game if you want.  Highly recommended for anyone who likes the cyberpunk genre or action rpgs.  My only gripe is that there isn't a third-person mode, since 1PP games tend to give me motion sickness.  The various 3PP mods aren't really a substitute for native 3PP, given that the game wasn't designed for it and they're all consequently some level of janky.

Take your time, take your sweet, sweet time with this game.
One of the worst features is a sensation of emptiness after completing all the side content, sincerely, take your time with Cyberpunk, get into it, make a character, do the whole spiel, roleplay by yourself, who's watching you?

Cyberpunk 2077 is an incredibly good game, and on a strong system it performs very well while looking visually outstanding. Night City is one of the most detailed and atmospheric open worlds I have played, with its lighting, reflections, city design, and overall presentation making it feel genuinely alive.

What really pushed the visuals even further for me was the **RenoDX HDR mod**. With RenoDX, the game looks absolutely stunning. The HDR feels far more impactful, with deeper contrast, brighter highlights, and a much more cinematic image overall. Neon signs, dark streets, interiors, car lights, and the skyline all look incredible, especially on an OLED display.

The game itself is also very engaging. The story, characters, side missions, and overall world-building all do a great job of keeping you invested. It feels like a game that rewards you for taking your time, exploring, and actually paying attention to the world around you.

Performance was also very good in my experience, especially considering how visually demanding the game can be. With the right settings and setup, it delivers a smooth and impressive experience without taking away from the visuals.

Overall, *Cyberpunk 2077* is absolutely worth playing, especially now with the improvements it has received over time. If you have a good HDR display, I would highly recommend trying it with RenoDX, because it makes an already beautiful game look even better.

I played Cyberpunk 2077 on my PS5 through PlayStation Plus and completed the entire game. After finishing it, I can confidently say that this game is a masterpiece.

The story is absolutely amazing, with memorable characters, emotional moments, and a world that keeps you invested from start to finish. The gameplay is also fantastic, combining action, RPG elements, exploration, and player choice in a way that feels both innovative and incredibly fun.

At launch, the game had performance issues and quite a few bugs, and the criticism was understandable. However, the developers have done an excellent job improving the game over the years. Almost all of the major bugs and performance problems have been fixed. A few minor bugs still remain here and there, but they are rare and don't affect the experience much. No game can be completely perfect.

Night City is one of the most immersive and detailed open worlds I have ever experienced. The visuals, soundtrack, atmosphere, and storytelling all come together to create something truly special.

In my opinion, Cyberpunk 2077 is one of the greatest games ever made and easily has a top-five story in video game history. I highly recommend it to anyone who enjoys story-driven games. This is a true masterpiece.

One of the few games that proved me wrong.

I tried Cyberpunk 2077 five different times. Every time, I played for a few hours, got bored, and quit. I genuinely didn't understand why so many people called it a masterpiece.

Then I forced myself to keep going.

The moment everything changed was Jackie's death. For the first time, the story truly grabbed me, and from that point on I couldn't stop playing. What started as a game I kept uninstalling eventually became one of my favorite games of all time.

Night City is one of the most immersive worlds ever created. Every district feels alive, every character feels real, and the atmosphere is unmatched. The graphics are incredible, the soundtrack is unforgettable, and the side quests are often better than entire stories in other games.

But what makes Cyberpunk special are the characters. V, Johnny, Panam, Judy, Jackie, Reed, Songbird...  Their stories stay with you long after the credits roll.

By the end, I wasn't thinking about builds, weapons, or missions. I was thinking about the choices I made and the people I met along the way.

A game I almost never experienced because I quit too early.

I'm glad I gave it one last chance.

The Witcher 3 is my favourite game of all time, I chose not to play Cyberpunk on launch due to all the optimization issues and bugs involved at the time, despite having faith CD-P-R would deliver something worth my time.
I finally got around to playing it and finished it today. I didn't enjoy it as much as the Witcher, copies of the same exact NPC will walk past each other in the same enclosed space, dialogues will overlap one another as though the characters are having an argument, sometimes even getting stuck in a loop. Debris and certain objects will spazz out and explode when you come into the slightest bit of contact with them. 
But the story is riveting, the characters are memorable and quests are unique. 
There are soundtracks that make you feel badass or make you bear the emotional weight of the moment as it plays. 
Combat is good, the graphics are better, and urguably my favourite feature of the game is the texting system. Even after you finish a quest and expect to never see or hear from a character again they'll text you in gratitude or just update you on their life. Its that small but significant detail that makes Night City feel alive, like you're not the only one living out their story.
If you haven't got around to it give this game a shot, it's well worth your time.

But it added to the immersion. Friend of mine told me there was fast travel in the game, but I'd always prefer to drive to places sometimes even walk. NPCs don't feel like passerbys and seem like they actually have a story to tell being in the city.

Got Phantom Liberty and coughed at how small the new area is (Dogtown). Was surprised that its density was hidden vertically with layers and pockets of places to explore.

The characters in the game was what made the game stick to me like caramel. You get an initial vibe from them, til you do their missions and you slowly start to see their true colors. The writing for each character is great that you'll either love them, hate them, or struggle finding out if you love them or hate them.

I played without subtitles to read peoples faces and eyes. Could see when people were lying, opening up, closing up.

Roleplayed as myself "what choices would I make if I was in this situation in Night City?". Made choices I regret and left things dangling on the clothesline that a min/maxer would've DM'd me for. But it taught me just how unforgiving Night City is. You scootch along anyway.

RPG of the decade (GTA 6 isn't an RPG right?).
If you didn't play this, you're missing out.

Was drawn away from this game for so long because of the bugs upon the first launch, but boy did they redeem themselves!!!

Despite this game's extremely messy launch CDPR has turned this game is one of the best open world story driven RGPs I've ever played, each life path feels unique with how it opens up the game before the heist, and each ending has its own emotional weight and finality to it. Most of the main companion stories are compelling, but all but one them feel kinda rushed. Phantom Liberty is a masterclass on what expansions should be and has a different feel from the main games story, but doesn't feel out of place within the wider world of Night City. 
However the game Isn't perfect cars spawn in upside down, certain areas of the game I've had my car just fall through the map, during River's questline I had to reload a save cause the quest wasn't progressing properly. Im sure there are more bugs, but these are ones that stood out to me.

All in all Cyberpunk 2077 started out as a mostly unplayable buggy mess and transformed into one the games best Ive ever played. If you like any of CDPRs other games or open world story driven RPGs, I would highly recommend grabbing this game and its DLC, however this game goes on sale fairly often( at the time of writing the summer sale should start in couple days) so it might me better to wait for a discount.

I want to preface this by saying that I have well over a hundred hours in this game on other platforms so dont be fooled by me "Meager" amount here.

Cyberpunk 2077 (Or CP if you want to risk it) is one of, if not, the best video game ive ever played in my life and might even be better than most movies Ive watched. It is a deeply personnal and tragic story supported by a dense, incredibly immersive world and cast of characters that I cannot stop coming back to. 

Its a sad, often very depressing story about loss, impending inevitable death and love in a world that doesnt welcome it anymore. Its main quest is, no joke, one of the most elegantly crafted story ive experienced, it honestly manages to make you feel every emotion its aiming for wether its making you feel like the coolest guy to ever live or to crush you with a deep sadness.

The gameplay is also fantastic, it is far, FAR, better than at launch and now includes a pletora of playstyles wether you want to fight with your boots on the ground and tank every shot, zip around arenas with five air dashes and slow down time with a katana or creep around with hacks that send people to literall digital hell.

It also suprisingly easy to mod which isnt suprising from the studio that made The Witcher 3 but is still very much appreciated

Overall my final score is a crisp 0/10 because they didnt let me crack Johnny🙏

Let me start off be saying this game is phenomenal. Elden Ring is nothing short of a masterpiece. From the moment you step into the lands between, the game captivates you with its vast, mysterious open world that rewards curiosity and exploration at every turn. The art style and immersive atmosphere of a usual Fromsoftware game combined with a extremely massive world makes for one hell of an experience. When you play Elden Ring you can see the passion, soul and effort they put in to it. They took everything they learned from previous titles and expanded on it.
 
My biggest issue with the game are the side quests. I don't mind not having a quest log to follow but it's literally impossible not to miss quests or mess others up by progressing too far without a guide and even with a guide it's still convoluted. I think Fromsoft got too used to their games being linear where you would likely find the NPC again and pretty soon at that. Elden Ring's format makes it not only very difficult to stumble upon them again you constantly risk breaking side quests by talking to the wrong people, killing a boss in the wrong order, or even just opening some other quest before you were "supposed" to. 

An average Fromsoft quest is like you find a wizard in the middle of nowhere and they tell you some gibberish like “follow the forsaken path” then you’re expected to find a secret chest in some swamp area that leads you to a note that assumes you’ll know how to go back to. Then you have to talk to an NPC 3 more times in 3 different locations to complete the quest. Then you find their corpse slumped over on a rock... It’s mind numbing. 

Another problem are the boss fights some of them have long and aggressive combos without any means to counter them without taking dmg except to move far away and let them finish their 5+ hit combo. Bosses like this are more suitable for Sekiro fight mechanics as defending against them doesn't consume stamina and is also another way of offense against them as well. Sekiro has the most refined combat system they’ve ever made, and if Fromsoft wanted Elden Ring’s bosses to be as fast as Sekiro, they should’ve allowed for the deflect/visible stamina mechanic to be utilized. 

Elden Ring is one of my favorite Fromsoft games to date. Exploring the lands between for the first time was the best experience in gaming i’ve ever had. But thinking of replaying it's quite a chore due to the sheer size of it, especially after just finishing it. The game needs either something like the bonfire ascetics from DS2 or the boss refight and boss rush from Sekiro. I don't want a reward or anything. I wouldn't even care if you didn't get runes. Just the option of redoing bosses without replaying the entire damn game would be a huge benefit.

[h3]A generational game, but not without flaws[/h3]

To preface this review: my only experience with any souls-like was 'The First Berserker: Khazan', and I never finished it.
Actually, I almost never finished Elden Ring either. A few hours in, and I was ready to quit, but the sheer number of players glazing this game was bugging me. "What do they see, that i dont?''
I couldn't tell you exactly when I found my answer; it was more of a gradual experience. But I do now understand, even if I don't fully agree.

The [b][i]world[/i][/b] that's built here is amazing. The ambience and overall vibes made me feel like this was a living, breathing realm full of despair and danger. Even without many cut scenes, I could still grasp the severity and gravity of the events that had taken place. (even if most of the time i had no idea what was happening in the story)

The [b][i]combat[/i][/b], while at times was a little frustrating, is still one of its best features. The build diversity and scope are something special that I can't really find a comparison to in anything else I've played before it. 
The bosses, too, were imaginative and challenging, and more than a few times I found myself jumping out of my seat and slamming my desk in excitement after I defeated them.

There's more I could praise, but I won't, because so many reviews have already done that. So instead ill bring up a few of the [b][i]negatives[/i][/b] that i dont see mentioned very often.
The target locking in this game is dreadful. I swear I died more times to this than actual bosses. It's mostly fine when you have a single enemy to deal with, but as soon as there are multiple targets, it fails miserably.
The other massive flaw I feel this game has is with its in-game systems/mechanics. I can't think of a single game I've ever played that required me to tab out and Google as many times as this. It really was ridiculous. There is just so much that isn't explained or explained well, and most players will miss out on a lot of content or waste a lot of time without Alt-tabbing and turning to guides. 

Overall though, I'm happy I put the time in and finished it. It was a great adventure.

This game did something VERY important for me, and it is that it brought me back from gaming burnout TWICE. 
The first time was when it came out, I just didn't want to play any games, everything felt bothersome, and then this game came out. BAM, instantly reignited my interest in video games. 
I was feeling this way again at the start of 2025, replayed the game for the third time, and BAM once again I felt my gaming itch return, and since then I've been on a crazy gaming binge. 
If I ever feel that way again, I know I'm gonna replay this game. Despite being open world and there being a trillion open world games out there, this one really stands out to be, you can truly just do whatever the living fk you want, and you'll have fun. 

Also, the world of Elden Ring to this day has been very inspiring to me for my own writing. It tells a lot by simply showing you, and I think that's something more games need to get better at.

SILLY LORE/PLAYED BEFORE KNOWLEDGE I never played a soulslike before, and before this game the usual combat in game experiences I had were (in order) Zelda, Baldur’s gate 3, and assasin’s creed Origins. Oh, and some light dragons dogma 2. I heard of Eldenring shortly after completing Baldur’s gate 3 and was intrigued by its alleged amazing worldbuilding handled by none other than George R.R Martin. (known from ASOIAF/GOT) This is kinda how the ball began to roll, but it would take YEARS before I would give Eldenring an actual chance. Mainly: Allegedly, ER is a very massive, beautiful but challenging game. Up until joining an achievement hunter discord, where tons of folks told me just how fun it was and that I should give it a try. Before, my biggest challenge in games used to be that a villager would plant its butt down in animal crossing in the way of my terraforming and oh my, has that issue been overshadowed immensely by Elden Ring.

OVERRAL THOUGHTS ABOUT THE GAME.
DESIGN:
The game is in my honest opinion, but I don’t play many games like this very beautiful designed. The colours seem lush and lifely, while still keeping that wandering between two worlds kinda element, as if you are in a magical place beyond our world. I liked the watergraphics, the grass looked cozy as the guards knocked me face down into it, and I am sure my character enjoyed bleeding out on some of the amazingly beautifully designed bridges. The character creator was also very important to me, and I sure enjoyed making my EldenLord a few times over…maybe too many times…

THE GAME FOR ME
So as I said, the game is unlike anything I played before. From the leveling system, to the rather harsh punishments when you do lose, to the scary enemies and the darker themes this is not a classical game I would normally pick up to play. And maybe that is exactly what made playing this (eventually) so much fun! At the first few times I felt like I was failing really badly and that I’d never make it past the first boss but after help from my friends and kinda learning that soulsgames=dying and learning I slowly became more confident. Bosses that used to send me running, or jumpscares that caused me to close the game became tests to test my reflexes and with every victory (big or small) I regained more and more confidence and learned along the way. The world that once seemed so overwhelmingly big became smaller as I uncovered pieces of the map and once my weapon was upgraded, and I actually paid attention to what a boss does during a fight, I had an absolutely almost unhealthy amount of joy playing it, jumping enemies in a violent way and screaming that their time had come, making concerned neighbors of mine wonder if everything was truly alright. Is ELDENRING for everybody? I think not, but what game truly is? It was a challenge, and it did what it promised. It challenged, it changed the way I’d approach a fight in a game and maybe even me along the way. 

10/10 would recommend.

Elden Ring took everything that made Dark Souls great, dialled it up a bunch of notches, and set you loose in an open world.

I wasn't initially sold on the idea of an Open world souls game, but I can honestly say that I think it was a fantastic move to make the genre accessible to new players. Far more so than in the souls series, if you hit a wall you can often pick another direction very easily, find new dungeons or encounters, and hunt for loot and runes without specifically banging your head against a wall. Pair this with the spirit ashes allowing summons in almost every boss fight that you can level with you, and this is a great starting point for newcomers to the genre.

If you're a veteran, the game has you covered too, since there's no NEED to engage with summons if you don't want to. The fights escalate a lot as the game goes on, and really make you feel rewarded for learning the dances in a way I feel is only beaten by Sekiro in the Genre.

The premise of Elden Ring was really quite simple. You know this iconic series of challenging action games FromSoftware is known to make? Take that and make it bigger, grander and epic. Make it Dark Souls, but open world. Then give GRR Martin yet another excuse not to finish the Winds of Winter by hiring him to create for you a backstory full of weird incestuous genderfluid gods. Finally, add to the mix a strange horse-goat of a mount and you have something really quite unusual. That's the premise and the promise. Now whether it delivers on that promise or not that's a different discussion. One that after spending a good 200 hours on this game's expansive world, perhaps I can answer.

Let's begin with the elephant in the room. The open world. This is one damn big elephant, truly ginormous. It goes on and on. Mountains, swamps, frozen over lakes, caverns, vast underground locations. This is one of the bigger games I have played and it's filled with details. It's also terrifyingly beautiful. The rich use of colour, the stunning landscapes, the dramatic vistas, the incredible heights. The game's art direction is top-notch. I would argue it's era defining. However there is an important caveat to consider. The open world is an incredible achievement yes, but how well does it serve the game's other mechanics.

Here the picture becomes somewhat less celebratory. You see the game retains its Dark Souls bones, the nuanced, environmental story-telling, the sparse quests driven by occasional meetups. Only now this spans a vastly larger surface and becomes a lot harder to experience naturally. Put simply, a great number of quests are next to impossible to do correctly without a guide. Not because of the minimalist design and lack of compasses, but exactly because of how incongruous the quest design is when applied to an open world. You execute a step in a quest. The character moves. Where? Go figure. In Dark Souls progression was linear and this was not much of an issue. Here, it is.

On gameplay this is largely Dark Souls still. Except significantly easier. This is likely another side-effect of the open world direction. I'm not saying the game is not challenging. Rush ahead and you will be punished. However, the game rewards careful exploration, as it rewards skilful combat. Generously. And there is plenty of rewarding side-content to conquer. Do that enough and by the endgame even superbosses won't be that scary. On a smaller scale, a disappointing element was the complete lack of a swimming ability. So many inviting islands close to the coast that are impossible to reach without gimmicks. The moment your character reaches the darker shade of waters down they go, as if they reached the proverbial end of the world. Be that as it may, this is a competently made and entertaining action game. Given its pedigree, I would expect no less.

Technically speaking the game is less impressive than its stellar art direction might suggest. Its graphical prowess in particular is decent but not extraordinary. There are sloppy technical decisions present that I wouldn't expect to see in a modern game. For example the lack of ultra wide monitor support. Even Dark Souls Remastered had that. But not Elden Ring? What's worse, the game is taking anti-cheating a bit too seriously and attempting to mod the game to include missing functionality can mark you as a cheater. I ended up having to play the game completely offline and with the use of extraneous software that didn't integrate quite seamlessly enough. All so that I could make the game work properly with my setup. FromSoftware really needs to do better here.

To conclude, does Elden Ring deliver on its promise. Is this Dark Souls but open world and is that a good thing? The first question is easy. Yes, it definitely is. The minimalist story telling, the challenging combat, the well crafted world. It's all here. The second question is harder. Dark Souls is not an open world game and making it one required changes that occasionally felt like discounts to the formula. In the end this is a lovingly made game of incredible ambition. It is beautiful and it is flawed. Is it worth playing? Absolutely, it's also worth pondering. There is something truly monumental just a few improvements away.

Gorgeous open world with deep lore and a great visual representation that tells the story on its own. The typical FromSoftware combat mechanics are on point. The bosses are fun to face, and due to the game being open-world, it is up to the player to choose which boss to fight next.

10/10 — a frenzied masterpiece.

Elden Ring is one of the most impressive open-world RPGs ever made. Developed by FromSoftware and directed by Hidetaka Miyazaki, the game combines the challenging combat the studio is known for with a massive world full of exploration, secrets, and unforgettable encounters.

The Lands Between is a stunning setting that constantly rewards curiosity. Every region feels unique, from haunting castles to underground cities hidden beneath the world. Unlike many open-world games, exploration rarely feels repetitive because there is always something interesting around the next corner.

Combat is deep and satisfying, offering a huge variety of weapons, spells, and character builds. Boss battles are a major highlight, requiring skill, patience, and strategy. While the difficulty can be intimidating for newcomers, the game's open structure allows players to explore elsewhere and grow stronger before tackling tougher challenges.

The story is mysterious and often told through environmental details, item descriptions, and character interactions. This approach won't appeal to everyone, but it creates a sense of discovery that fits the game's world perfectly.

The few drawbacks include occasional performance issues and a lack of traditional quest tracking, which can sometimes leave players confused about where to go next. However, these flaws are minor compared to the overall experience.

Overall, Elden Ring is a masterpiece that successfully blends challenging gameplay, rewarding exploration, and rich world-building. Whether you're a longtime fan of FromSoftware games or a newcomer looking for an unforgettable adventure, it is a game well worth playing.

I was one of those people who bought it as my first souls game shortly after release. I knew I was supposed to skip bosses and explore, but I wandered into some late game zone, got bodied repeatedly, felt annoyed and just put it down.

But it's mentioned everywhere, so I had to give it another go. This time I looked up a cheesy build and didn't care - all I wanted was to actually experience it. Around the 15 hour mark it all started to click, and boyyy did this game suck me in.

The sense of wonder is something I hadn't felt in years. Going over a hill and seeing something massive in the distance, knowing you can just go there - no quest marker, no hand-holding, just you and your curiosity. Just so much hidden stuff you find that leaves you with a sense of wonder.
Honestly, Elden Ring ruined other games for me.
I still haven't touched the DLC - I'm saving it like a drug addict hiding away that one last dose.

If I HAD to nitpick: there is little to no indication how to complete quests and you will miss a lot of stuff, I read the wiki a lot because I had no idea how to finish a side quest. Levelling - some Turbo Knight you spent 20 minutes trying to beat will drop a penny, meanwhile a wolf in a wolf pack in another area that dies in 2 seconds will dump far more.

All in all really I can say is give it time. Let it click. Then let it take you away. One of the best experiences in recent gaming history.

Elden Ring is one of the greatest games I've ever played. From the moment I stepped into Limgrave, I was completely hooked by the massive open world, incredible atmosphere, and sense of freedom. Unlike many open-world games, exploration actually feels rewarding because there's always something exciting to discover—whether it's a hidden dungeon, a powerful weapon, a terrifying boss, or an entirely new area.

The combat is fantastic and gives players endless ways to build their character. Whether you prefer giant swords, magic, katanas, or a mix of everything, the game lets you play exactly how you want. The boss fights are some of the most memorable in gaming, offering intense challenges that make victory feel genuinely satisfying.

Visually, Elden Ring is stunning. Areas like Liurnia, Leyndell, and the underground cities are breathtaking and constantly made me stop just to admire the scenery. The soundtrack perfectly complements the world, making every major battle and discovery feel epic.

What truly makes Elden Ring special is the feeling of adventure. It constantly encourages curiosity and rewards players for exploring every corner of its world. Even after finishing the game, I found myself wanting to start another playthrough to try new builds and discover things I missed.

Elden Ring isn't just an amazing RPG—it's a masterpiece that sets a new standard for open-world games. If you enjoy exploration, challenging combat, and unforgettable worlds, this is a game you absolutely must play.

Rating: 10/10

If you were lured here because you think this is an open world similar to BOTW, no it isn´t the world only opens up bit by bit and you only really get to decide in what order you do things in if you´re not on your first playthrough because the way you get to bosses is structured very linear in a way that you can only get around in if you know about small and hidden side paths (this is only in the first half of the game, the second half of the story is linear with no possible diversion in the steps you complete the story in). This game is also really not something for people who like to have things be completed or orderly because it is not possible to get everything in one consecutive playthrough (excluding the new Journey feature) due to some items being locked out because of story or quest progression without letting you know. this brings me to my next issue of quests etc. not being marked or progressed clearly leading to me having to constantly use a wiki for quests because they are barely followable without any external guidance.
This all being said I really like the lore and world building which has been created within this world. The characters were designed and thought out well especially looking at characters like Rykard which have extensive and thorough hidden lore for you to explore I mostly enjoyed my time in this game.

In conclusion if you like challenging games or enjoy learning about characters / worlds this is a game for you. If you don´t like those things or are a cleanly person I would not recommend this game for you (seriously I spend hours working on getting every weapon and item and I am still not done).

Elden Ring throughout the years of my playing it has left me disappointed and wanting to play Fromsoft's previous works. You spend most of this game riding around on an annoying horse just to fight bosses that are poorly balanced and are not that great after you take away all the particle effects. Navigating the world can only really be enjoyed on the first playthrough as it is just large swaths of nothing. I much prefer the more linear style of Fromsoft with emphasis on level design so take the previous criticism with a gain of salt, and Elden Ring does have a few dungeons that are designed well enough. Combat itself has taken a dip in quality because the enemies and bosses simply don't abide by the same laws you do anymore; with long drawn out combos, input reads and insane gap closes.

I have not finished the DLC. It is just everything I dislike about the game taken to a further extreme.

Legendary game. A masterpiece of a singleplayer game, with a bit of co-op and pvp thrown in there too.

The only real critique I personally have, is that the game is not playable at above 60 fps. I feel like today that is an actual issue, as most people with good PCs are playing games at 120+ fps and it is a big difference in smoothness and visual quality imo. 

Nevertheless, I used Lossless Scaling app to activate framegen and can play it at 120 fps that way, it works perfectly. After you have played the game once, I also highly recommend a remake mod called Elden Ring Reforged. It makes the experience better and is very easy to install. 

This game is a 10/10.

Good game! Great visuals, great combat, insane level of flexibility to the point that 60% of the game can be considered optional if you're good enough. Loot progression is immensely satisfying, the game make sures to make every mechanic, consumable etc useful enough that nothing feels useless. Huge replay value too, I finished the game on a strength/faith build but am already excited to try again focusing on a mage build, the freedom is crazy.

Legacy dungeons were easily my favourite part about the game, lots of hidden shortcuts and treasures with fun progression through various wild bosses, the game really encourages you to explore the world without it feeling like a constant grind. Most of all though the sheer scale of imagination was astounding, beautiful underground cities and massive mega castles  on mountaintops etc, starry night skies, cool autumn vibes, theres even a completely optional part of the map thats in a constant blizzard which is really surreal.

First time fromsoft player and its easily a solid 9/10, challenging boss fights can be made much easier by exploring the world and finding better loot etc and then coming back, to the point of sometimes nerfing yourself just to feel more challenged. My only pet peeve was the inconsistent narrative structure, the worldbuilding was incredible I just wish they utilised cutscnes more and that side quests didn't feel so cryptic sometimes. You'll have to pull up online wikis to backtrack steps and progress side quests which gets annoying but you'll get used to it, good thing is theres load of good guides online anytime you feel lost. Anyways hope you enjoy!

THE AMAZING:
First and foremost, this is one of the best open worlds I have ever expierenced. Every vista was beautiful, there was always something to go find on the map and do. I'd get a map fragment and then immedietly mark like 5 points of interest to go visit, be pleasantly surpised and do it again. 

It was easy to get immersed in the world, and nearly every single piece of armor or weapon excited me in some way that made me want to try different builds. 

THE MEDIOCRE
Quests. Holy hell this game has obtuse quests. THey don't really give you any direction to go, and half the time you'll sit there confused as you try and figure out where to go or who to talk to. This doesn't become as much of an issue when you're exploring everything. 

THE BAD
Now even though the game is difficult, there is a difference between difficulty and some of the absolute ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ this game has to offer. Some things that were done - Valient Gargoyles, duo bosses overall, and a few areas - feel like an afterthought or just slapped together. Some things are frustrating or exist for frustrations sake. 

OVERALL

"One of the best gaming experiences of my life.

Elden Ring is, without a doubt, one of the most beautiful and immersive games I have ever played. The creature designs are absolutely stunning, and the world is filled with legendary items and secrets that constantly keep you engaged.

What makes this game truly special is its incredible variety. You are never restricted to a single playstyle; you have the freedom to build your character exactly how you want, using countless combinations of weapons, spells, and gear. Almost anything you need to create your own legend is at your fingertips.

However, it is not a perfectly flawless game—it does have its share of technical and design imperfections. But despite those minor flaws, the scale, depth, and sheer artistry of this world make it one of the greatest games I have ever seen. Highly recommended."

After about 70 hours in Elden Ring, I can confidently say it's one of the most impressive games I've ever played. It takes the challenging combat and rewarding exploration that FromSoftware is known for and puts it into a massive open world that constantly encourages curiosity.

The world itself is the game's greatest strength. Almost every time I saw something interesting in the distance, I could actually go there and discover a dungeon, boss, hidden item, or completely new area. Exploration rarely feels like a waste of time because there is almost always something meaningful to find.

Combat is difficult but satisfying. Boss fights can be incredibly challenging, but learning attack patterns and finally overcoming a boss after multiple attempts is one of the most rewarding feelings in gaming. The variety of weapons, spells, and builds also gives players a lot of freedom to play however they want.

Visually, the game is stunning. While it isn't the most technically advanced game available, its art direction is incredible. Areas feel unique and memorable, from golden fields to terrifying underground cities. The soundtrack also helps make major boss encounters feel epic.

That said, the game isn't perfect. Some late-game bosses can feel frustrating rather than fair, and the open world occasionally reuses enemies and dungeon layouts. The story is also presented in a very indirect way, which some players may love but others may find confusing.

Even after 70 hours, I still feel like there are secrets I haven't discovered and areas I haven't fully explored. Few games manage to maintain that sense of mystery for so long.

Rating: 9.5/10

Elden ring is an interesting continuation of the franchise. I have played through 3 times now (Twice on PS, now once on PC) and my personal take is: The open world is ok, I'm personally not a huge open world gamer and Elden Ring does not move the needle IMO. A lot of dead and empty space or mediocre rewards (mushrooms, cookbooks, etc.) and very little reactive or emerging events make the map feel a little underwhelming to me. The best parts of the game are the linear/non-linear traditional souls style levels (the legacy dungeons, the underground rivers), and those more linear areas are some of the best in the franchise. Gameplay is good as always with some welcome additions, changes, and re-introductions (power stancing). I personally feel ever since DS3 the series is becoming a little too boss fight focused and the boss fights are getting a little too crazy with the delayed attacks and mix ups and just too much aggression compared to how our characters play. Elden Ring in particular has a couple of big offenders (malenia, about half the duo fights, most of the DLC) but there's also some amazing bosses here like mogh, godfrey, bayle, and so on. 

Overall I like the game but don't find myself replaying it as much or as often as older games in the franchise, particularly for how expansive the map is and the time commitment

Did you play Ocarina of Time as a child and think Hyrule field was huge? That the dungeons were a little scary but so fun to explore? The bosses towered over you but you found a way to beat them? But that feeling of exploring and finding new weapons, completing dungeons, and advancing the quest, it's all here. Elden Ring is the greatest fantasy adventure game of all time.

FromSoft gets lost in the sauce a bit towards the end in terms of difficulty/player expectation.

I also think this would've better without the open world, instead having a traditional Souls world design.

Overall great game though, 9/10.

Despite seeing people play and talk about this game very frequently before playing it, I find myself forgetting things constantly that I knew because it's very complicated. Certain things are strangely opaque, like the way the item and ability descriptions love to speak in riddles or frequently describe nothing at all (I specifically don't like how the game yelled at me when I opened it after closing the window to stop playing the first few times since I couldn't find the save and quit button), and the game is as hard and frustrating as it looks, definitely the most frustrating game I've ever played. Despite all of this, I have always welcomed a challenge or some charming jank in my games, and the game rewards you for learning to live the potential power/struggle fantasies it presents with all of the possible play styles; as much as it punishes you for overestimating yourself or making false assumptions of how easily you can do anything.

What makes this game so special is not just it's mind-blowingly and possibly unmatched ambitious scope and size, but it is the build variety that can make this game still feel fresh and challenging each time you play it. The 590 hours on my account does not reflect the additional 2000+ hours I have played on console, and I have managed to make a different build in each of my 15+ playthroughs that changes the way I have to approach boss and enemy encounters.  The possibilities are ENDLESS.  Holy, lightning-throwing paladin? check. Slice and dice dex build? check. Frost wizard? check. Dragon-channeling fire breather? check. "Hulk smash" tank? check. Want to make a completely unique combo of any of these? You can do that too! The amount of weapons and spell combos are staggering. The DLC adds even more to this, and is large enough to be a whole game on it's own. It is a 100% must play if you enjoy the base game.

The lore and world-building is rich and has depth and mystery to it.  The voice acting is superb. The environments and enemy design are stunningly beautiful. The music is epic. The characters are unique and there will be ones you love or loathe. The bosses are beautifully realized and challenging. The community interactions, co-op, and PvP can be both hilarious, maddening, and rewarding. There is just not enough good things I can say about it this game. It deserved all of the awards and praise it received upon release, and will endure as one of the best games ever made.
