I've waited a long time to bring RDR2 back to life on a powerful rig so I can breathe life into the Old West.  RDR2 is simply one of the greatest pieces of fiction ever made and is especially important for me.  RDR2 released eight years ago and brought genuine joy in one of the worst times in my life.  I owe Rockstar a piece of my soul for something so precious as this.  The game is Rockstar's greatest work and I don't think it could ever be topped.

(+)  I have always preferred single-player stories for games, talented actors and superb scripts soaked in a rich and vibrant world.  The game being a prequel to RDR1 was a stroke of genius as foundations can be placed and a lot of the history of the Old West can be pre-baked before going into the original.  The Van Der Linde gang has been explosively expanded and has brought a really colourful cast of characters to the heart of the story.  The visuals and the execution of its art direction is stunning, the game is coming up to eight years old but the way gold can glint in the sun, how rain can cut through clouds and how blood can pop and pour over mud was just a glory to witness.  The effort and talent of Rockstar is on full-display and the game is mostly a technical marvel that is all puppeted beautifully and always animated elegantly.
 The chemistry between the actors is really felt and I especially think Benjamin Byron Davis is a knock-out for Dutch perfectly representing the games theme, however, the whole gang is excellent and memorable.  Arthur of course was instantly famous and is arguable one of the best leading men ever written and Roger Clark deserves his flowers for being top of the call sheet for one of the best ensembles ever casted.  I love the storybook feeling of the games chapters and how you swoop over the different biomes.  The smoky rain that follows the thunder across sunset is all dynamic and the game really feels alive.  The cities and towns are dense and thick with life and there are tonnes of games, parlours and shops to interact with and I lost a lot of hours in just enjoying the world.  The map is huge and diverse and the game fully lets you ride into the vistas and engage in side plots with strangers and freaks up and down the map.  I love how the soundtrack lifts up a lot of the story-telling and especially how it glazes over the ambience of the world when you're riding around.  The sound of the world is excellent and the punchiness of the weapons is great, especially when married with smoke that bursts out the barrels in the gunfights.  I've played this game half a dozen times on PS4 and I still get lost and fall in love with the game time and time again, like falling into the pages of your favourite book.  I think the game has a perfect pace and how as you near the conclusion the whole thing starts picking up an inevitable momentum and you really feel the desperation in trying to preserve something like how a wave can wash away a mark in the sand.  I absolutely love the narrative and always think its powerful when a game's story can be so heart-breaking that you feel pain in wishing things were different and how it always hurts feeling the loss of something so real.  The game is strict in its mission design and you are an actor on Rockstar's stage but I feel the pay-off in the games chapter finales especially make up for keeping up on a tight track and a lot of the missions have great gunfights, story beats and locations but mostly all three.

(-)  I was a little shocked at the port here.  I don't remember it fritzing as much as it did on PS4.  I often had textures, especially some of the rendering flicker and I had a couple of soft locks with its performance.  I got the game up on its feet after a bit of tinkering under the hood but I expected this to be flawless and it was just something I never stopped noticing in my time.  Something with Rockstar specifically I don't like is being graded for my mission performance, I think its a poor way of incentivising replaying missions.

Overall, this game will always have a special place in my heart and I owe this game a lot for pulling me through an awful time in my life.  The game is a love letter to the Old West and an ode to how video games are the greatest story-telling devices with an enrapturing setting and a wonderful ensemble.  You will fall in love with this masterpiece and I can't think of a game I would recommend more to anymore more than Red Dead Redemption 2.  I completed 85% of the content in 80 hours and I will continue to pour my life into something I love so dearly.

Not much to say that hasn't already been said. I bounced off this game twice before finally committing to it and I'm glad I did. The story is great, as is the world building, freedom, visuals, and overall feel of being a cowboy in a gang during this time period. It takes a while to get used to controlling Arthur, but once you do it becomes second nature. I can see why some may be turned off by the slow pace and there isn't really a way around that, but if you can hang up that hat then it's worth it to enter this world. Performance on my computer (upper mid-range aging CPU / high-end GPU in 2026) is fantastic and I only played with a wireless Xbox One controller.

*Edit - I forgot to mention that the amount of time that you spend with your horse was a surprising welcome to me. I knew that horses were the means of transportation during that time but I did not expect to spend so much time taking care of them, making sure they were clean, fed, happy, etc. It was not a burden at all, the horse animations and sounds are great; I treated them better than Arthur to be honest!

After playing Red Dead Redemption 2, I can confidently say it delivers one of the best stories in gaming.
The narrative is exceptional, with incredible character development that keeps you emotionally invested from start to finish. The evolution of each member of the gang is gripping and often unpredictable. There are moments of intense drama that make you sympathize with them at times, and feel frustration with their choices at others, which makes the story feel very human and layered.
Gameplay is a bit heavy and slow-paced, but it feels intentional and well-balanced with the game’s realism. It may not be for everyone, but it adds weight to every action you take.
The realism and world design are outstanding. The environment feels alive and deeply immersive, encouraging exploration at every turn. Even minor NPCs and secondary characters react naturally to the world and change depending on circumstances, which makes the world feel dynamic and believable.
Overall, it’s an incredible experience and one of Rockstar’s greatest achievements, with a story that easily stands among the best in gaming history.

People that say this game plays like a movie are entirely right. This game is a cinematic masterpiece in the best way possible. If you're willing to take your time with a game's narrative, you will love this. Not to mention, there still is a very high amount of action in this game, just isn't happening 24/7. Top complaint though, ♥♥♥♥ Saint Denis. I don't entirely hate the place, but I tried hard to not hold a bounty during the game and they made it incredibly difficult there.

I'll start with the stuff I least talk about. RDR2 is very immersive with gorgeous graphics. It's story is a very well told one. An outlaw named Arthur Morgan following and questioning his posse's leader and finding redemption near the end of the story.

Alright now onto the actual game. I'm going to get out the bad parts of the game out of the way. The horse riding can be very janky half the time, there are some very annoying collectibles/stranger missions (mainly the hunting request and the exotic mission). You can't complete all challenges until the epilogue. There are a few challenges that are grindy or tedious. The Wanted system can be very sensitive and BS at times. There are missable stranger missions, items, and animals. Most of the items and money you gathered/looted and saved are gone when you get to the epilogue. There is no NG+. At camp you move REALLY slowly. Moose is a fkin mythical creature in the game. Horses stats mostly don't matter at all. Money is kind of useless once you got the weapons and outfits you want. Deadeye makes the game too easy due to it slowing down time and auto reload every time you enter deadeye. No poncho or sombrero. Legendary animals are underwhelming. You're only able to craft items one at a time.

Other than that everything else about the game is great and very fun. RDR2 is one of the best Wild West theme game ever. The game is very immersive like I said and a cinematic experience. Sometimes you're an outlaw doing illegal stuff, a bounty hunter, an animal hunter, or a fisherman. Half of the game and time I spent playing were just fishing/hunting animals for clothes and upgrade camp I do not need. Other times I'm just picking flowers (herbs) and spending time crafting items one at a time. What stands out to me is when you just traveling on horseback from A to B or doing just about anything, there will always be some random event/encounter that shakes the game up a bit. It is either people want to kill/rob you, people need help, people try to scam you, or people interacting with each other that you can interact/intervene with. And in the end, you decide what to do in all those situation, making it feel like an adventure. The world and NPCs feel alive and not just hollow NPCs walking around. The gunplay isn't the best but it is decent. There are various tools and weapons in the to mess around. It really does feel like a proper open world game that we rarely get nowadays. Of course an open world game isn't complete without collectibles littered all over the map. They're not as bad without a guide and is just running from point A to B.

Over all, 9.5/10. Despite the list of annoyances and gripes I have with the game, the story and immersive gameplay carried RDR2. Also fk u Dutch and Micah.

The standards of the video game industry are so low. It's playable. It's enjoyable all right. But it's not in any way shape or form a good video game. 

I just spend 5 minutes trying to open a chest, only for Arthur or whatever is name is to keep jumping on it. It's not me jumping on it, it's just autojumping to get to the wall, I think? And yeah, of course it needs auto jumping, because every other key on your keyboard does something very specific. Like, who do you brawl? Use F and R of course. How do you talk to people? Hold right click and then press E or F. What does F do? It's for punching people. Right. What does E do? It's for chocking people of course. Because of course you need a touch spefically for chocking people. And wait, I'm not even done. What does right click does? It's for talking to people, but it's also for aiming at them. You know time I lost because of some ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥, due to the worst keymapping I've ever seen? I love losing my perfect skin, just cause instead of pressing F, I pressed E. That's your perfect 10/10 game? A game that can't even manage the basics of video game? 

That game was never thought out as a game for PC, it should have never been on PC. And that's a shame, cause I'm sure if the key mapping wasn't that bad, it'd be a decent game. But that's just the base of video games. 

I get it, the broody cowboy is what every men wants to be. And what's better than one broody cowboy? A ton of them, speaking with the same Texas Accent. It's peak male fantasy, I get it.

I’ve delayed writing this review for a long time because it’s hard to put into words what Rockstar actually achieved here.
i have dedicate over 1k hours on my xbox.
Most games are built to be fun distractions. Red Dead Redemption 2 feels like a living, breathing ecosystem that you just happen to be allowed to exist in.

The World: It ruins other open-world games for you. If you shoot a bird, a coyote will come eat the carcass later. If you don't wash your clothes, townspeople will treat you like a pariah. You can spend three real-world hours just fishing, hunting, and watching the weather change across the valleys, and it never feels like "grinding."

Arthur Morgan: Easily one of the greatest protagonists ever written in fiction, period. His growth, his internal conflicts, and his loyalty are so masterfully handled that by the end of the journey, you don't feel like you played a character—you feel like you lost a friend.

The Pace: It's slow. Deliberately slow. It forces you to slow down, match its rhythm, and actually live in the late 1890s.

It’s been months since the credits rolled, and I still catch myself listening to the soundtrack on my commute. It’s not just a game; it's a monumental achievement in digital storytelling.

Buy it. Play it. Don't rush it. And keep some tissues nearby for Chapter 6.

I don't usually write reviews, but this game deserves one.

When I started Red Dead Redemption 2, I expected a good open-world western. What I got was one of the most memorable stories I've experienced in any game. The world feels alive in a way that still surprises me even after dozens of hours. Sometimes I'd set out to do a mission and end up hunting, fishing, helping strangers, or just riding around because the world is that immersive.

The real highlight is Arthur Morgan. He starts as a character you like, but by the end he becomes someone you genuinely care about. His journey, the relationships within the gang, and the way the story unfolds stayed with me long after the credits rolled.

The game isn't perfect. It's slow at times, and some animations can feel a little too realistic. But honestly, that's part of what makes the world feel believable.

I watched every cutscene and never felt the need to skip one. By the end, I wasn't rushing to finish the game—I didn't want it to end.

If you've somehow avoided spoilers and haven't played this yet, do yourself a favor and experience it. Games like this don't come around very often.

[b]The Good[/b]
Red Dead Redemption 2 offers one of the most immersive and authentic worlds ever created. The atmosphere is exceptional, the storytelling is rich, and several character arcs are genuinely memorable. The gunplay is solid, and the game truly shines when you slow down and let yourself sink into its cinematic, organic rhythm. It’s a world that feels alive, and it rewards players who take their time to live in it rather than rush through it.

[b]The Bad[/b]
On PC, however, the experience comes with... personality. Random crashes, visual glitches that pop in with the subtlety of a brick through a window, and the infamous Rockstar Launcher seems determined to test your patience. Physics tied to frame rate mean that playing above 60 FPS is basically asking the universe for a soft‑lock. And sometimes the controls decide that Arthur forgot how to draw his gun or how his legs work.

[b]The Ugly[/b]
The save system. Dear lord, the save system. Could it be the worst piece of software ever developed? It feels like it was designed by someone who had given up on the joy of living. It’s obnoxious, hostile and unpredictable, and it seems to actively sabotage your attempts to save progress. 

[b]Verdict[/b]
It didn't feel like one of the greatest games of all time to be honest, but despite all of that, I still recommend it... if you’re the kind of player who values story and immersion enough to tolerate the technical issues. Whether it’s worth it depends entirely on your taste and your patience, which will definitely be put to the test.

The best game I've ever played!!!

This game took me through every emotion possible (joy, stress, sadness, and awe). I loved how I could shape Arthur into my own version of the character, making him feel truly personal. That's one of the things I admire most about RDR2: every player's Arthur feels different, and there are endless ways to approach the world and its challenges.

I cried during the final mission, and the feeling of dread when starting the epilogue is something I'll never forget. Even hearing Jack's older voice hit me harder than I expected. After taking some time because my emotions were all over the place, I finally finished the epilogue and can confidently say this is a masterpiece.

Played for the hype, stayed for Sadie and Arthur.

The game is a comfort game to me as I sank into it when I was going through cancer treatment a few years ago. This was also a game that my sister and I really bonded over, that both of us got diagnosed with the same kind of cancer only a few weeks between each other.

Safe to say, the game means so much to me, and I hope other people will have the same wonderful experience as I did.

BEAUTIFUL world. BEAUTIFUL story-telling. Everything about it is beautiful, there are so many different interactions and missions (both story and non-story) that make everything about this game AMAZING. 10/10 would ditch my kids to play this game again.

While most of my hundreds of hours are on XBOX, I've had the pleasure of playing this title on several platforms. Being on my third playthrough, I can honestly recommend this game to anyone who looks for a rich story from their gaming experience. Story mode alone gives room for dozens of hours of playtime, hundreds if you make full use of the truly open-world setting you're placed in. Near endless random events coat this game head to toe, bringing the world to life far outside the main storylines. It feels easy to connect and even roleplay with the many characters present throughout the world, and each playthrough continues to feel like a breath of fresh air.

The opposite side of the coin, multiplayer, has its ups and downs. Like any multiplayer title there's only ever so much you can do before, eventually, some things become repetitive. I personally wish the multiplayer storylines were longer, or even if there were a new quest to be released by the dev team. Truly though, multiplayer is best experienced with a couple friends purely for the fun of wacky hijinx.

The greatest game of all time.  I have purchased it on multiple platforms, put hundreds of hours into it and I still find new stuff or cutscenes or interactions that somehow passed me by.  It forces the player to slow down and take stock of everything.  Your horse isn't just an animal but a true companion, your allies aren't faceless people but rather friends you know very well.  The missions feel personal and even the optional ones feel like you NEED to do them.  Arthur Morgan is one of the greatest game protagonists of all time for many reasons, his nuance, his struggle with his own nature as well as his place in the world.  You care about him and his journey in a way that many games cannot match.  

There is a reason why 8 years later, people STILL use this as a benchmark and compare graphics to new AAA titles.  It is a marvel on so many fronts.  The world feels alive yet empty at the right moments.  The animals feel like they belong and aren't just there for you to hunt.  The NPC's go about their days and complete tasks.  The lighting, the weather, it all feels purposeful and well created.  It's a masterpiece.  It has become my go-to mental health game, I pick it up when I need a boost.  It is always there for me.

The best story game ever made to this day period. There is no other like it, Dan Houser somehow perfectly had the player connect with Arthur Morgan more as a person than just a character you play as.  Hopefully GTA VI can make that same connection, Beautiful graphics by itself, not even mentioning mods like Visual Redemption, and the fact that Rockstar made the world of lemoyne feel so alive just contributes to the immersion even further.  Progression can feel slower than other story games, but I feel the slowness is necessary for the story to really sink in and play out. Games like this are why the video game industry far surpasses the movie industry. And the voice acting is also incredible, the voices match the characters appearance's so well, the music choices are beautifully timed and chosen. Arthur Morgan is one of the best written and overall characters the entertainment industry has ever seen and its not even close. And Dan Houser perfectly added this tension and a sense of the unknown as the story progresses. This game changed my perspective on life and so has it many others. Its a damn shame that Dan Houser will not be writing GTA VI. This game is perfect. Overall a beautifully cinematic experience. 12/10

There's definitely a good game in there, but it's hidden by a lot of trash and half-baked gameplay elements the developers put in the game only to annoy the player it seems.

The great thing about RDR2 is definitely its great open world with nice secondary characters, side quests and encounters.

The bad thing is... everything else [i](but I'll only mention the two main annoying bits)[/i]

The main story could have been great but drags on too long while forcing you to watch scripted events you can't do anything about, forcing you to watch the gang making the dumbest decision possible most of the time.

Everything you do takes at least twice the amount of time it should take, which is probably the reason a single run took me 85h. I reckon if we get rid of the unnecessary long riding bits during story missions and long-ass animations, I could have shoved off a good 20 hours of time from it.

Overall, it feels like there's two games: a great open world game and a bad story-focused game that should have been a more linear game instead.

The open world itself still makes me recommend it, but barely.

Red dead's story is super rich. In story mode, there exists so many characters and stories. The map is big and beautiful, and its easy to recognize which state you're in, by just looking around.
This game is a prequel, so the events of this game happen before red dead 1.
Playing the online mode could be some fun. But not many people play online and there are a good amount of modders. Online doesnt really get any updates cause rockstar hates rdr2 online for some reason. Online can be confusing to navigate and learn how it works. Im not a big fan of how online has handled gold, because its pretty hard to get. The gold currency is set up in the same way mobile games have a special currency, that is super hard to get and then tells you that you can buy some from the in-game store. The special gold currency does NOT exist in story mode.

Story mode has so much to do. From collecting things and giving them to people, to the many unique stranger missions.

One thing i reccomend when you play this game is that you SHOULD NOT rush it. Take your time to play the stranger missions and interact with the world. Various goods can also be found around the map.

I would absolutely reccomend this game. Have a good time cowboy!

Red Dead Redemption 2 is a game I came across multiple times before I started playing myself.
It was recommended to me by friends from horse-based games, my dad, my girlfriend, and even my college teacher. Which would be surprising, until you consider that it was a computer science course that dabbled in and out of games. The passion my teacher at the time had for the game surprised me a little, and I’m still not sure why I didn’t pick it up at the time.

The game itself has two elements:
On one hand, there’s the story mode where you play as Arthur Morgan. I haven’t played this version of the game personally, I did however watch my lovely girlfriend run a playthrough of it. 
On the other hand, it has an Online multiplayer version, with a customizable character and it’s own story independent from that of Arthur’s. This is the version I myself lean towards more. 

That playthrough of course, wasn’t her first time nor will it be her last. I fully believe she could quote the story beginning to end without a single stutter should someone ask. She’s currently standing at a strong 404 hours played, and that mostly comes down to the story. For comparison, I’m merely standing at 31 hours over the span of the past year. My girl has put me to shame.

She’s someone who’s deeply invested in this story, and has so much love for it. Expectedly, she was the one who swayed me to play the game too. 
I feel the pure excitement to play through the game more than once, while also having the ability to talk about why this game is good so extensively, is a massive indicator to how well it’s been written and put together.

The game provides you with so much in depth detail of characters, it almost creates a parasocial connection the more you go on. As someone who loves to see into other people’s minds and thoughts, the personal journal you as the player get to read through is such a nice detail. I really enjoyed stopping my love when a new entry appeared to have a little nosey through it. 

Turning to the online version however, is a completely different experience tied up into a similar format. I started out following my girlfriend around like a lost puppy, but got the hang of it pretty quickly. The game is super intuitive which is really nice. 

I enjoy an open-world game where I can mind my own business. While you still have the opportunity for quests and to follow a story, picking a specialist role and running with it has been so fun from the get-go. Naturally, my love favoured being a bounty hunter, but turning to Madam Nazar and becoming a collector caught my eye the most. While I haven’t had the opportunity to get too far with this as of yet, I’ve definitely been enjoying roaming around the game collecting items. 

Overall, I believe this game has so many opportunities for all types of players. 5 different Specialist roles all which focus on different aspects the game has to offer, and of course a fun and entertaining story. The graphics are beautiful and the whole game has been put together in such a stunning way.

This game has given me such nice conversations and time spent with my love, and the story has also given me some food for thought. I would definitely recommend this game if you haven’t decided to try it out yet.
I just played this game, Completesd the story including Epilogue. God sent this game. Masterpiece, you cant get better then this.
this is the only game for me where the npcs genuinely feels alive, interaction feels real, crazy graphic, and peak story. love ts 67676767676767
it's just a good game, funner than most games, pretty relaxing, cinematic. Gun play is decent, your walking might feel a bit heavy, but, easy to get used to.
Really this is the best game I have ever played in all way, storywise, gameplay wise, graphically etc. RDR2 IS GOATED GAME. GO FOR IT

without a doubt red dead redemption 2 best game on pc for sure from single player to multiplayer , just make sure you got friends to play with in multiplayer! fyi:  greatest open-world experiences ever made.

Back when RD Redemption 1 came out, people loved John. RDR2 came in and blew him out of the water with Arthur. This game took everything the first Redemption did and made it better. Phenomenal game.

This game is one of the best ever created. It's beautiful. Everyone cries when they finish this game because of how amazing it is. I have no words to fully express just how amazing and wonderful this game really is. From the literally hundreds of different animals to the customization of your outfit, guns, and horse, this is absolutely amazing. You can become a hardcore outlaw and rob and kill folk, or become a good person and help people. It's so much more than I have even said here, so please get it for yourself. It's worth every penny and more.
Online may suck but I still spent 200+ hours in just online mode, and I didn't spend single (real) cent on it.

In terms of pure gameplay, Naraka is a great and unique battle royale. The combat feels great, the characters and their abilities are well designed and fun to use. It essentially is fighting game style combat with a mix of moba / hero shooter esque character abilties and ultimates. It also offers an insane level of character customization where you can really go off on the details if you enjoy that.

However, the reality of the gameplay experience has a few glaring issues, mostly related to the game's lack of popularity in the west. Essentially 95% + of the playerbase is Chinese, playing on the CN server. The other servers have an extremely small population. It is extremely easy to become a top 500 player or a top 100 on the leaderboard of a certain character because the amount of people playing on the NA / EU servers is so small. ( That's why its funny that some of the people in these reviews are saying proudly that they're top 500, lol sweetie we all are.)

This lack of playerbase leads to the biggest issue of playing on NA servers: the queue times. In the earlier ranks, queues will be manageable because the lobbies are completely full of bots, but when you get to higher ranks they become ridiculous. Outside of peak gameplay times, I struggle to get games at all and regularly wait 15-30 min and straight up have my queue time out. 

During more popular times its still a dice roll whether the queue will pop within 10-15 min. When I do get into a game, its with the same 10 people who I see over and over, the top ranked people on the server because those are the only people who play the game in NA. This obviously gets tiring quickly. It's a shame because if the game had a healthy population in the west it would be a blast.

Its possible to play on the Chinese server with some workaround but if you are in the US the ping will be very high. However, the queue times are only 1-2 min at all times and the lobby is full of actual players. It creates a sad dilemma for NA/EU players, would you rather maybe get 1 game an hour on low ping or play regularly but with very high ping?

Based on the gameplay alone I recommend just checking it out, it is free, and even fighting bots is fun. I hope that it picks up steam in the west, However at this point I'm not sure I see that happening :(

I’ve played a lot of battle royale games over the years, but honestly, Naraka: Bladepoint feels like something completely different in the best possible way. Most BR games focus only on guns and positioning, but Naraka mixes fast-paced movement, intense melee combat, grappling hooks, mind games, and mechanical skill in a way that feels incredibly satisfying once you get into it.

At first, the game can feel overwhelming. There are so many mechanics to learn — combos, counters, focus attacks, movement tech, hero abilities, weapon matchups, and timing. But that’s exactly why the game feels rewarding. Every fight feels like an actual duel instead of just “who saw who first.” When you win a fight in Naraka, it genuinely feels earned.

One of my favorite things about the game is the movement system. The grappling hook alone makes traversal insanely fun. Flying through trees, chasing enemies across rooftops, escaping impossible situations, or suddenly turning a fight around with mobility never gets old. The game feels fast, fluid, and stylish almost all the time.

The combat is probably the strongest part of the game. It’s easy to understand the basics, but there’s a very high skill ceiling. Good players can outplay opponents through timing, reads, positioning, and smart decision-making instead of pure luck. Every weapon also has its own personality. Some weapons feel aggressive and explosive, while others reward patience and precision. Learning different weapons keeps the gameplay fresh even after many hours.

Visually, the game is beautiful. The environments, character designs, outfits, animations, and overall atmosphere are genuinely impressive. The art direction gives the game a unique identity compared to other battle royale titles. Some moments honestly feel like scenes from a martial arts movie.

Another thing I appreciate is that Naraka creates memorable moments constantly. Whether it’s surviving a chaotic 1v3, winning a duel with 1 HP left, landing a perfect counter, or swinging into a final fight out of nowhere, the game creates stories naturally. Few multiplayer games give me that adrenaline rush anymore, but Naraka still manages to do it.

Of course, the game isn’t perfect. The learning curve is brutal for new players, and sometimes matchmaking can feel rough when you run into extremely experienced players. There are also moments where server issues or ping differences can be frustrating. But despite those problems, the core gameplay is so strong that I keep coming back.

What really makes Naraka special for me is that it rewards improvement. The more time you invest, the more the game opens up. You start understanding mind games, spacing, combo routes, movement tricks, and team coordination on a deeper level. It feels satisfying to notice your own progress over time.

Overall, I genuinely think Naraka: Bladepoint is one of the most unique multiplayer games released in recent years. It combines skill-based combat, mobility, strategy, and cinematic action better than almost any other battle royale game out there. It can be difficult, frustrating, and competitive, but it’s also incredibly fun and rewarding once it clicks.

If you enjoy games with deep combat systems and high skill expression, I absolutely recommend giving Naraka a real chance.

Best combat system of any game ever in my opinion. Skill ceiling is hella high, which is the best part. Furthermore, the game also caters to casuals with the PVE gamemode. More gamemodes will keep getting added in the game, but what it really lacks is the global player count. Though I heard the chinese server has a much healthier player count.

Just imagine a fighting game where 3d positioning is included like a FPS game. That's this peak game, Naraka.

One of the most well balanced PvP games on the market. Further because of the way the game systems are designed its very easy to tell if players are using cronus to automate and augment their gameplay. All specs and characters and weapons are viable in all the forms of PvP. BG's, Battle Royale, PvE, and extraction game modes. This game has it all. It also has the NBPL Asian Esports league attached to it. Easy to pick up, hard to master. Works like a fighting game only its 3D with how the combo system works. Has ranged weapons as well that gives you a well rounded layout if you want to play ranged weapons as well. High movement, fast, pvp gameplay. You won't regret getting into this game.

This game's combat is honestly some of the most fun i've ever had the pleasure to engage with, pulling off combos & parries feels amazing, the cosmetics are REALLY pricey at times but that's all optional stuff, characters are pretty easy to unlock & there's one for pretty much every playstyle, not only that, but you've got a lot of freedom for playstyle expression with alternate abilities even on the same character, it's a lot of fun.

I’ve played a lot of battle royale games over the years, but honestly, Naraka: Bladepoint feels like something completely different in the best possible way. Most BR games focus only on guns and positioning, but Naraka mixes fast-paced movement, intense melee combat, grappling hooks, mind games, and mechanical skill in a way that feels incredibly satisfying once you get into it.

At first, the game can feel overwhelming. There are so many mechanics to learn — combos, counters, focus attacks, movement tech, hero abilities, weapon matchups, and timing. But that’s exactly why the game feels rewarding. Every fight feels like an actual duel instead of just “who saw who first.” When you win a fight in Naraka, it genuinely feels earned.

One of my favorite things about the game is the movement system. The grappling hook alone makes traversal insanely fun. Flying through trees, chasing enemies across rooftops, escaping impossible situations, or suddenly turning a fight around with mobility never gets old. The game feels fast, fluid, and stylish almost all the time.

The combat is probably the strongest part of the game. It’s easy to understand the basics, but there’s a very high skill ceiling. Good players can outplay opponents through timing, reads, positioning, and smart decision-making instead of pure luck. Every weapon also has its own personality. Some weapons feel aggressive and explosive, while others reward patience and precision. Learning different weapons keeps the gameplay fresh even after many hours.

Visually, the game is beautiful. The environments, character designs, outfits, animations, and overall atmosphere are genuinely impressive. The art direction gives the game a unique identity compared to other battle royale titles. Some moments honestly feel like scenes from a martial arts movie.

Another thing I appreciate is that Naraka creates memorable moments constantly. Whether it’s surviving a chaotic 1v3, winning a duel with 1 HP left, landing a perfect counter, or swinging into a final fight out of nowhere, the game creates stories naturally. Few multiplayer games give me that adrenaline rush anymore, but Naraka still manages to do it.

Of course, the game isn’t perfect. The learning curve is brutal for new players, and sometimes matchmaking can feel rough when you run into extremely experienced players. There are also moments where server issues or ping differences can be frustrating. But despite those problems, the core gameplay is so strong that I keep coming back.

What really makes Naraka special for me is that it rewards improvement. The more time you invest, the more the game opens up. You start understanding mind games, spacing, combo routes, movement tricks, and team coordination on a deeper level. It feels satisfying to notice your own progress over time.

Overall, I genuinely think Naraka: Bladepoint is one of the most unique multiplayer games released in recent years. It combines skill-based combat, mobility, strategy, and cinematic action better than almost any other battle royale game out there. It can be difficult, frustrating, and competitive, but it’s also incredibly fun and rewarding once it clicks.

If you enjoy games with deep combat systems and high skill expression, I absolutely recommend giving Naraka a real chance.

full of chinese smurfers everywhere but if you dont play competitively and try the other game modes as well its a lot of fun, i also like the graphics and how versatile facial customization is. you can make anybody

In terms of pure gameplay, Naraka is a great and unique battle royale. The combat feels great, the characters and their abilities are well designed and fun to use. It essentially is fighting game style combat with a mix of moba / hero shooter esque character abilties and ultimates. It also offers an insane level of character customization where you can really go off on the details if you enjoy that.

However, the reality of the gameplay experience has a few glaring issues, mostly related to the game's lack of popularity in the west. Essentially 95% + of the playerbase is Chinese, playing on the CN server. The other servers have an extremely small population. It is extremely easy to become a top 500 player or a top 100 on the leaderboard of a certain character because the amount of people playing on the NA / EU servers is so small. ( That's why its funny that some of the people in these reviews are saying proudly that they're top 500, lol sweetie we all are.)

This lack of playerbase leads to the biggest issue of playing on NA servers: the queue times. In the earlier ranks, queues will be manageable because the lobbies are completely full of bots, but when you get to higher ranks they become ridiculous. Outside of peak gameplay times, I struggle to get games at all and regularly wait 15-30 min and straight up have my queue time out. 

During more popular times its still a dice roll whether the queue will pop within 10-15 min. When I do get into a game, its with the same 10 people who I see over and over, the top ranked people on the server because those are the only people who play the game in NA. This obviously gets tiring quickly. It's a shame because if the game had a healthy population in the west it would be a blast.

Its possible to play on the Chinese server with some workaround but if you are in the US the ping will be very high. However, the queue times are only 1-2 min at all times and the lobby is full of actual players. It creates a sad dilemma for NA/EU players, would you rather maybe get 1 game an hour on low ping or play regularly but with very high ping?

Based on the gameplay alone I recommend just checking it out, it is free, and even fighting bots is fun. I hope that it picks up steam in the west, However at this point I'm not sure I see that happening :(

I’ve played a lot of battle royale games over the years, but honestly, Naraka: Bladepoint feels like something completely different in the best possible way. Most BR games focus only on guns and positioning, but Naraka mixes fast-paced movement, intense melee combat, grappling hooks, mind games, and mechanical skill in a way that feels incredibly satisfying once you get into it.

At first, the game can feel overwhelming. There are so many mechanics to learn — combos, counters, focus attacks, movement tech, hero abilities, weapon matchups, and timing. But that’s exactly why the game feels rewarding. Every fight feels like an actual duel instead of just “who saw who first.” When you win a fight in Naraka, it genuinely feels earned.

One of my favorite things about the game is the movement system. The grappling hook alone makes traversal insanely fun. Flying through trees, chasing enemies across rooftops, escaping impossible situations, or suddenly turning a fight around with mobility never gets old. The game feels fast, fluid, and stylish almost all the time.

The combat is probably the strongest part of the game. It’s easy to understand the basics, but there’s a very high skill ceiling. Good players can outplay opponents through timing, reads, positioning, and smart decision-making instead of pure luck. Every weapon also has its own personality. Some weapons feel aggressive and explosive, while others reward patience and precision. Learning different weapons keeps the gameplay fresh even after many hours.

Visually, the game is beautiful. The environments, character designs, outfits, animations, and overall atmosphere are genuinely impressive. The art direction gives the game a unique identity compared to other battle royale titles. Some moments honestly feel like scenes from a martial arts movie.

Another thing I appreciate is that Naraka creates memorable moments constantly. Whether it’s surviving a chaotic 1v3, winning a duel with 1 HP left, landing a perfect counter, or swinging into a final fight out of nowhere, the game creates stories naturally. Few multiplayer games give me that adrenaline rush anymore, but Naraka still manages to do it.

Of course, the game isn’t perfect. The learning curve is brutal for new players, and sometimes matchmaking can feel rough when you run into extremely experienced players. There are also moments where server issues or ping differences can be frustrating. But despite those problems, the core gameplay is so strong that I keep coming back.

What really makes Naraka special for me is that it rewards improvement. The more time you invest, the more the game opens up. You start understanding mind games, spacing, combo routes, movement tricks, and team coordination on a deeper level. It feels satisfying to notice your own progress over time.

Overall, I genuinely think Naraka: Bladepoint is one of the most unique multiplayer games released in recent years. It combines skill-based combat, mobility, strategy, and cinematic action better than almost any other battle royale game out there. It can be difficult, frustrating, and competitive, but it’s also incredibly fun and rewarding once it clicks.

If you enjoy games with deep combat systems and high skill expression, I absolutely recommend giving Naraka a real chance.

In terms of pure gameplay, Naraka is a great and unique battle royale. The combat feels great, the characters and their abilities are well designed and fun to use. It essentially is fighting game style combat with a mix of moba / hero shooter esque character abilties and ultimates. It also offers an insane level of character customization where you can really go off on the details if you enjoy that.

However, the reality of the gameplay experience has a few glaring issues, mostly related to the game's lack of popularity in the west. Essentially 95% + of the playerbase is Chinese, playing on the CN server. The other servers have an extremely small population. It is extremely easy to become a top 500 player or a top 100 on the leaderboard of a certain character because the amount of people playing on the NA / EU servers is so small. ( That's why its funny that some of the people in these reviews are saying proudly that they're top 500, lol sweetie we all are.)

This lack of playerbase leads to the biggest issue of playing on NA servers: the queue times. In the earlier ranks, queues will be manageable because the lobbies are completely full of bots, but when you get to higher ranks they become ridiculous. Outside of peak gameplay times, I struggle to get games at all and regularly wait 15-30 min and straight up have my queue time out. 

During more popular times its still a dice roll whether the queue will pop within 10-15 min. When I do get into a game, its with the same 10 people who I see over and over, the top ranked people on the server because those are the only people who play the game in NA. This obviously gets tiring quickly. It's a shame because if the game had a healthy population in the west it would be a blast.

Its possible to play on the Chinese server with some workaround but if you are in the US the ping will be very high. However, the queue times are only 1-2 min at all times and the lobby is full of actual players. It creates a sad dilemma for NA/EU players, would you rather maybe get 1 game an hour on low ping or play regularly but with very high ping?

Based on the gameplay alone I recommend just checking it out, it is free, and even fighting bots is fun. I hope that it picks up steam in the west, However at this point I'm not sure I see that happening :(
